+| Decomposition entry hub | Sibling cluster bodies + dependency entries | Assemble a decomposed module (see **Module Decomposition**) |

The CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh` (via `BURD_BUK_DIR`). All other module and config sourcing happens inside the furnish function, after the `buc_doc_env` / `buc_doc_env_done` gate. This enables doc-mode help display without runtime dependencies.

+The **decomposition entry hub** is the one implementation file permitted to source at file top level: a decomposed module's `«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh` sources its guard-free cluster bodies (and any decomposed dependency's entry) at top level, not inside a function. An undecomposed implementation file still sources nothing outside its kindle. This carve-out exists only because the entry owns the single inclusion guard for the whole module — see **Module Decomposition**.
## Eval Policy
`eval` is **forbidden** except for validated variable-name dereference. Require `^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$` regex validation before any eval.

content.text: Now the **Module Maturity Checklist** — three of its Module Structure bullets are literally wrong for decomposed modules (CLI suffix, guard placement, top-level sourcing). I'll correct those inline and add a decomposed-module sub-block:

input.old_string:
### Module Structure
- [ ] Implementation file «prefix»_«name».sh exists
- [ ] CLI file «prefix»_cli.sh exists (skip if library/utility module with no direct user commands)
- [ ] Implementation has multiple inclusion detection guard (`Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED`)
- [ ] CLI starts with `set -euo pipefail`
- [ ] Implementation file sources nothing (except within kindle function)
- [ ] CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`; all other deps sourced in furnish
- [ ] External code accesses module through CLI only (no direct `z*_kindle()` calls from outside)
- [ ] `_cli.sh` files are executable; all other `*.sh` files in the module directory are not
input.new_string:
### Module Structure
- [ ] Implementation file «prefix»_«name».sh exists (decomposed: container prefix names no file — see Decomposed Modules below)
- [ ] CLI file «prefix»_cli.sh exists — `«prefix»0_cli.sh` if decomposed (skip if library/utility module with no direct user commands)
- [ ] Implementation has multiple inclusion detection guard (`Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED`) — decomposed: only the entry hub carries it; clusters are guard-free (except a cluster sourced by more than one entry)
- [ ] CLI starts with `set -euo pipefail`
- [ ] Implementation file sources nothing (except within kindle function; a decomposition entry hub also sources its clusters at top level)
- [ ] CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`; all other deps sourced in furnish
- [ ] External code accesses module through CLI only (no direct `z*_kindle()` calls from outside)
- [ ] `_cli.sh` files are executable; all other `*.sh` files in the module directory are not

### Decomposed Modules (when applicable)
- [ ] Container prefix names no file — every file carries a `0` or gesture-letter marker (`«prefix»0_*`, `«prefix»«g»_*`); none is bare `«prefix»_«name».sh`
- [ ] Dispatch functions keep the bare container prefix (`buc_execute «prefix»_ …`, `«prefix»_«command»`) — terminal exclusivity is filesystem-level, not function-level
- [ ] Entry is `«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh` (not the `k`-postfix interim form — see FM-002); holds the single `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED` guard, the whole kindle, and the sentinel; non-executable
- [ ] Cluster bodies `«prefix»«g»_«Name».sh` are guard-free and sourced exactly once by the entry; they read kindle constants as module-wide globals
- [ ] CLI furnish sources the entry hub, then calls `z«prefix»_kindle`
- [ ] `«prefix»0_cli.sh` is the only executable file; entry and clusters are not
- [ ] Files live flat in the kit directory — no subdirectory for a single decomposed module

content.text: Now **FM-002** in the Fading Memory section — the bridge for a maintainer who opens a `k`-form entry and needs the canonical form. Generic notation, no rb sites (those are consumer-kit-specific):

input.old_string: **Migration:** When touching a file that uses the old pattern, transform to new. Enroll calls must move into kindle. Return variables must use `z_«funcname»_«retval»`. Arrays must gain `_roll` suffix using `z_«prefix»_` convention. Accessors must gain `_recite` suffix.
input.new_string:
**Migration:** When touching a file that uses the old pattern, transform to new. Enroll calls must move into kindle. Return variables must use `z_«funcname»_«retval»`. Arrays must gain `_roll` suffix using `z_«prefix»_` convention. Accessors must gain `_recite` suffix.

### FM-002: `k`-entry → `0_«ModuleName»` entry

The first module decompositions named the entry hub with a `k` postfix (`«prefix»k_«Name».sh`) before the `«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh` form was settled. The `0`-tier entry is now canonical (see **Module Decomposition**). This convention never appeared in BCG proper — it is an interim form that arose in practice; FM-002 records it so a maintainer who finds a `k`-entry recognizes the lag and knows the target.

**Superseded pattern:**
- Entry file: `«prefix»k_«Name».sh` (e.g. a `…k_Kindle.sh`), holding the single inclusion guard and the whole kindle
- The `k` postfix marked "the kindle entry"

**Recognition — legacy code looks like:**
- A `«prefix»k_*` file carrying the single `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED` guard and the complete `z«prefix»_kindle`, sourced by a sibling `«prefix»0_cli.sh`
- A decomposed module whose entry does not sort adjacent to its `0_cli` in a directory listing

**Current convention:**
- Entry file: `«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh` — identical role (single guard + whole kindle + sentinel), renamed so entry and CLI share the `0` top tier and sort together
- The `k` gesture letter is freed for ordinary cluster use

**Migration:** Rename `«prefix»k_«Name».sh` → `«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh` and update the one `source` line in `«prefix»0_cli.sh`'s furnish. Pure rename plus one sourcing-path edit; no behavior change. Migrate opportunistically — do not bulk-rename.

=== 'implementation file' / single-module assumptions ===
37:Every module has an implementation file. CLI entry points are only present if module requires standalone execution.
121:Module Architecture above assumes one module fits in one implementation file. When a module outgrows that — the single file grows past comprehension, or distinct gesture-clusters within it acquire independent mass — the module **decomposes** across several files while remaining one module. Decomposition is load-bearing only when the single file genuinely no longer fits: a module that fits in one file stays in one file (Load-Bearing Complexity).
1499:The **decomposition entry hub** is the one implementation file permitted to source at file top level: a decomposed module's `«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh` sources its guard-free cluster bodies (and any decomposed dependency's entry) at top level, not inside a function. An undecomposed implementation file still sources nothing outside its kindle. This carve-out exists only because the entry owns the single inclusion guard for the whole module — see **Module Decomposition**.

This carve-out exists only because the entry owns the single inclusion guard for the whole module — see **Module Decomposition**.

1682:- [ ] Implementation file sources nothing (except within kindle function; a decomposition entry hub also sources its clusters at top level)
1687:### Decomposed Modules (when applicable)

1692:- [ ] CLI furnish sources the entry hub, then calls `z«prefix»_kindle`
1998:### FM-002: `k`-entry → `0_«ModuleName»` entry
2000:The first module decompositions named the entry hub with a `k` postfix (`«prefix»k_«Name».sh`) before the `«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh` form was settled. The `0`-tier entry is now canonical (see **Module Decomposition**). This convention never appeared in BCG proper — it is an interim form that arose in practice; FM-002 records it so a maintainer who finds a `k`-entry recognizes the lag and knows the target.

content.content:
119	Enrollment types and gating API: see `buv_validation.sh`.
120	
121	### Module Decomposition
122	
123	Module Architecture above assumes one module fits in one implementation file. When a module outgrows that — the single file grows past comprehension, or distinct gesture-clusters within it acquire independent mass — the module **decomposes** across several files while remaining one module. Decomposition is load-bearing only when the single file genuinely no longer fits: a module that fits in one file stays in one file (Load-Bearing Complexity).
124	
125	**The container prefix names no file.** Once a module decomposes, its prefix becomes a *container* — the function namespace and the sort-root for the file family, but no file is named `«prefix»_«name».sh` with the bare prefix. Terminal exclusivity here is a **filesystem** invariant, not a function-namespace one: the dispatch functions keep the bare container prefix (`«prefix»_«command»`, `buc_execute «prefix»_ …`) even though every *file* carries a tier marker. A reader greps `«prefix»_` for behavior and `«prefix»0_`/`«prefix»«g»_` for files.
126	
127	Decomposition has two tiers.
128	
129	**Top tier — `«prefix»0_`** (the `0` sorts it to the head of an `ls`):
130	
131	| File | Role | Executable | Guard |
132	|------|------|------------|-------|
133	| `«prefix»0_cli.sh` | CLI gateway — `z«prefix»_furnish`, `buc_execute «prefix»_ …` | yes | — (CLI header) |
134	| `«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh` | Entry — the single inclusion-guard, the whole kindle, the sentinel | no | `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED` |
135	
136	The entry is the module's **sourcing hub**: it holds the one `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED` guard and the complete `z«prefix»_kindle` / `z«prefix»_sentinel`, and it sources the cluster bodies. The CLI's furnish sources the entry (alongside the module's dependencies) and then calls `z«prefix»_kindle` — the CLI-as-gateway contract is unchanged; only the file furnish reaches is now the entry hub rather than a lone `«prefix»_«name».sh`. The entry is not the CLI: the executable gateway stays in `«prefix»0_cli.sh`.
137	
138	**Cluster tier — `«prefix»«g»_«Name».sh`** (gesture-letter bodies):
139	
140	Each cluster is a body file carrying one coherent group of the module's functions, named with a gesture letter (`«g»`) that scopes the group. Clusters are **guard-free**: the entry sources each exactly once, so a per-cluster inclusion guard would be inert ceremony. Clusters read the entry's `readonly Z«PREFIX»_*` kindle constants as globals — kindle runs in the entry, the constants are visible module-wide.
141	
142	**The single-guard rule, and its one exception.** Exactly one file carries `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED`: the entry. Clusters omit it because they are sourced once, by the entry alone. The exception is a cluster sourced by **more than one** entry — e.g. a primitives cluster shared between a module and an independent consumer that sources it directly. A multiply-sourced cluster keeps its own `Z«PREFIX»…_SOURCED` guard, because the "sourced exactly once" premise no longer holds. The guard tracks *how many entries source the file*, not the tier.
143	
144	**Sourcing.** Decomposition adds one sanctioned sourcing site beyond the three in Sourcing Rules: the entry hub sources its sibling cluster bodies (and, where a dependency is itself decomposed, that dependency's entry). This is the kindle graph made physical — see **Sourcing Rules**.
145	
146	**Filesystem marker.** The `_cli.sh`-is-executable invariant is unchanged: `«prefix»0_cli.sh` carries `+x`; the entry `«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh` and every cluster do not. Decomposition adds files but no new executables.
147	
148	**Topology — flat by default.** A single decomposed module's files live flat in the kit directory alongside undecomposed modules; the `0`/gesture markers already group them in a listing. A subdirectory is reserved for a *family of distinct modules* grouped for cohesion (each with its own `«prefix»0_cli` + entry) — never for the parts of one decomposed module. One module decomposing is not grounds for a subdirectory.
149	
150	`«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh` is the canonical entry form. An earlier interim form postfixed the entry with `k` (`«prefix»k_«Name».sh`); see **Fading Memory FM-002**.
151	
152	---
153

file.content:
Enrollment types and gating API: see `buv_validation.sh`.

### Module Decomposition

Module Architecture above assumes one module fits in one implementation file. When a module outgrows that — the single file grows past comprehension, or distinct gesture-clusters within it acquire independent mass — the module **decomposes** across several files while remaining one module. Decomposition is load-bearing only when the single file genuinely no longer fits: a module that fits in one file stays in one file (Load-Bearing Complexity).

**The container prefix names no file.** Once a module decomposes, its prefix becomes a *container* — the function namespace and the sort-root for the file family, but no file is named `«prefix»_«name».sh` with the bare prefix. Terminal exclusivity here is a **filesystem** invariant, not a function-namespace one: the dispatch functions keep the bare container prefix (`«prefix»_«command»`, `buc_execute «prefix»_ …`) even though every *file* carries a tier marker. A reader greps `«prefix»_` for behavior and `«prefix»0_`/`«prefix»«g»_` for files.

Decomposition has two tiers.

**Top tier — `«prefix»0_`** (the `0` sorts it to the head of an `ls`):

| File | Role | Executable | Guard |
|------|------|------------|-------|
| `«prefix»0_cli.sh` | CLI gateway — `z«prefix»_furnish`, `buc_execute «prefix»_ …` | yes | — (CLI header) |
| `«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh` | Entry — the single inclusion-guard, the whole kindle, the sentinel | no | `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED` |

The entry is the module's **sourcing hub**: it holds the one `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED` guard and the complete `z«prefix»_kindle` / `z«prefix»_sentinel`, and it sources the cluster bodies. The CLI's furnish sources the entry (alongside the module's dependencies) and then calls `z«prefix»_kindle` — the CLI-as-gateway contract is unchanged; only the file furnish reaches is now the entry hub rather than a lone `«prefix»_«name».sh`. The entry is not the CLI: the executable gateway stays in `«prefix»0_cli.sh`.

**Cluster tier — `«prefix»«g»_«Name».sh`** (gesture-letter bodies):

Each cluster is a body file carrying one coherent group of the module's functions, named with a gesture letter (`«g»`) that scopes the group. Clusters are **guard-free**: the entry sources each exactly once, so a per-cluster inclusion guard would be inert ceremony. Clusters read the entry's `readonly Z«PREFIX»_*` kindle constants as globals — kindle runs in the entry, the constants are visible module-wide.

**The single-guard rule, and its one exception.** Exactly one file carries `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED`: the entry. Clusters omit it because they are sourced once, by the entry alone. The exception is a cluster sourced by **more than one** entry — e.g. a primitives cluster shared between a module and an independent consumer that sources it directly. A multiply-sourced cluster keeps its own `Z«PREFIX»…_SOURCED` guard, because the "sourced exactly once" premise no longer holds. The guard tracks *how many entries source the file*, not the tier.

**Sourcing.** Decomposition adds one sanctioned sourcing site beyond the three in Sourcing Rules: the entry hub sources its sibling cluster bodies (and, where a dependency is itself decomposed, that dependency's entry). This is the kindle graph made physical — see **Sourcing Rules**.

**Filesystem marker.** The `_cli.sh`-is-executable invariant is unchanged: `«prefix»0_cli.sh` carries `+x`; the entry `«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh` and every cluster do not. Decomposition adds files but no new executables.

**Topology — flat by default.** A single decomposed module's files live flat in the kit directory alongside undecomposed modules; the `0`/gesture markers already group them in a listing. A subdirectory is reserved for a *family of distinct modules* grouped for cohesion (each with its own `«prefix»0_cli` + entry) — never for the parts of one decomposed module. One module decomposing is not grounds for a subdirectory.

`«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh` is the canonical entry form. An earlier interim form postfixed the entry with `k` (`«prefix»k_«Name».sh`); see **Fading Memory FM-002**.

---

content.text:
Done — the section reads as a coherent unit and the ripples are reconciled. Here's what landed in `BCG-BashConsoleGuide.md`, all in synthetic notation, no new rb-grafting:

**The deliverable (docket's three scope items):**
1. **`### Module Decomposition`** centerpiece (end of Module Architecture) — container-prefix-names-no-file, two-tier layout, single-guard rule + the multiply-sourced-cluster exception, sourcing, filesystem marker, flat-default topology.
2. **Module Maturity Checklist** — corrected the 3 Module-Structure bullets that were literally wrong for decomposed modules (CLI suffix, guard placement, top-level sourcing) + new **Decomposed Modules (when applicable)** sub-block.
3. **FM-002** (`k`-entry → `0_«ModuleName»`) in Fading Memory.

**Whole-document reconciliation (scope item 1):** Sourcing Rules gained a 4th row + entry-hub carve-out; Naming table gained 3 decomposed file-form rows; a forward pointer from the top of Module Architecture; the Decision Matrix row. Filesystem marker needed no change — it already classifies the new files correctly, which the section states explicitly.

**Three judgment calls I made — please sanity-check, these are where I'm least certain:**

1. **Cluster file case = PascalCase** (`«prefix»«g»_«Name».sh`). Matches your cinched `0_«ModuleName»` and the rbld/rbfc practice — but it *diverges* from BCG's existing single-file rule ("lowercase, prefer one word"). The rbh0 family uses lowercase clusters (`rbhob_base`), so the tree is itself split on this. If you'd rather clusters follow the lowercase impl rule, it's a one-table-row + one-checklist-line change.

2. **rbh0 omitted from BCG entirely.** Per "not sure we have healthy archetypes" + "don't graft to rb," BCG now documents *one* clean pattern (the kindle-hub model). rbh0's kindle-in-`base` divergence is treated as rbk-local, not a BCG variant — it isn't mentioned. Confirm you're good with it being silent rather than reconciled.

3. **FM-002 has no "Known legacy sites" list** (FM-001 does). The actual `k`-form sites are all rbk-specific (`rbldk_`, `rbfck_`, `rbflk_`) and listing them would graft rb into the distributed guide — so I used generic recognition signatures instead.

**Deliberately *not* done (would be scope creep — flag if you want any):** a worked "Template 4: Decomposed Module" (the file/role table covers it); de-grafting BCG's pre-existing rb examples (`rbrn_`, `rbob_`); a pointer line inside Module-CLI Prefix Unity.

No tests apply (it's a `.md`; nothing to lint or build). I did **not** touch heat/pace state and did **not** notch — files are uncommitted for your review. The pace's Done criteria look met pending your read of the three calls above.

Want me to adjust any of those, or are we good to wrap `₢BUAAA`?

content.text:
Wrapped — `₢BUAAA` complete, commit `07aae4425` (state-transition only; tree was already clean from your foundry commit).

One thing I'd flag before you act on that `retire BU` hint: **I'd hold off retiring `₣BU`.** The "all paces complete" signal is technically true but misleading here — `₢BUAAA` was the *misfiled* BCG pace, not the heat's actual purpose. `₣BU`'s paddock is your deliberate **release-prep durable memory** ("paddock-only and stabled until the project is close to actually cutting a release"). Retiring would archive that release model and the pending pristine-run / RELEASE.md work right when you're *not* releasing. The heat is already stabled — which is exactly its intended resting state. My recommendation: leave `₣BU` stabled, don't retire. With the BCG pace gone it's back to being the clean paddock-only release heat its paddock describes.

Your call, of course — if you *do* want it retired for some reason I'm not seeing, say so and I will.

Still open whenever you want them (not blocking anything): the three judgment calls from the draft — cluster-file **PascalCase vs lowercase**, **rbh0** left silent in BCG, and **FM-002 without a sites list**. They're recorded in the wrap summary so they won't be lost.

echo

content.text: My grep was broken — and I need to flag that honestly before going further. The `--include=*.sh` got stored unquoted in a variable, so zsh glob-mangled it and grep scanned **nothing**. Those zeros were false negatives, not a clean codebase. `buc_die hits: 0` is the tell — it's all over the tree. Re-running correctly, and folding in your "_cli against peers" steer, which is the right lens:

input.command:
ROOT=/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster
echo "############ SANITY (corrected quoting) ############"
printf 'buc_die hits: '; grep -rn 'buc_die' --include='*.sh' "$ROOT/Tools/rbk" "$ROOT/Tools/buk" | wc -l
echo
echo "############ EACH *_cli.sh : top-level (header) source lines ############"
echo "BCG: CLI header sources ONLY buc_command.sh; all other deps in furnish."
echo
for f in $(find "$ROOT/Tools/rbk" "$ROOT/Tools/buk" -name '*_cli.sh' | sort); do
  hdr=$(grep -nE '^source ' "$f")
  printf '=== %s\n%s\n' "${f#$ROOT/}" "$hdr"
done

lastPrompt: Now that you are smart about the current BCG wording, can you detect violations?  I'll slate a repair pace.

content.text: Now grep is real, and your "_cli against peers" trick paid off immediately. Two more drilldowns before I synthesize — the 55 `2>/dev/null` hits need scope-filtering (Cloud Build step scripts under `rbgj*/` aren't BCG modules), and the bare-`>` hits need context to separate genuine SC2188 truncations from continuation-line redirects:

input.command:
ROOT=/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster; R="$ROOT/Tools/rbk"; B="$ROOT/Tools/buk"
echo "############ 2>/dev/null — IN-SCOPE only (exclude GCB step scripts rbgj*, ABANDONED, FUTURE) ############"
grep -rn '2>/dev/null' --include='*.sh' "$R" "$B" | grep -vE '/rbgj[a-z]/|ABANDONED|/FUTURE/' | sed "s#$ROOT/##" 
echo
echo "############ context: rbfcp_Plumb.sh bare-> (truncation vs continuation?) ############"
sed -n '338,344p;380,384p' "$R/rbfcp_Plumb.sh"
echo "---- rbfr_FoundryRetriever.sh:88-92 (is > a continuation redirect?) ----"
sed -n '86,92p' "$R/rbfr_FoundryRetriever.sh"
echo "---- rbldb_Bole.sh:178-186 ----"
sed -n '178,186p' "$R/rbldb_Bole.sh"

lastPrompt: Now that you are smart about the current BCG wording, can you detect violations?  I'll slate a repair pace.

content.text:
The sweep is done and triaged. The bare-`>` hits all turned out to be continuation-line redirects of multi-line `jq`/`curl` (not SC2188 truncations), and most `=$(...)` were arithmetic `$(( ))` — filtered out. Here's the real findings list, scoped to actual BCG modules (Cloud Build step scripts under `rbgj*/` and `vov_veiled/{ABANDONED,FUTURE}` excluded as non-module — flag if you disagree).

## Headline — your `_cli`-vs-peers trick

**`buym_yelp.sh` sourced at the CLI header.** BCG (boilerplate table + Sourcing Rules + Template 2): *"CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`."* But **~24 of 33 CLIs** also source `buym_yelp.sh` at the header; only 9 source `buc_command.sh` alone. When the dominant practice contradicts the wording, it's usually the **doc** that's stale — `buym_yelp` likely needs to be blessed as a permitted second header source (for doc-mode yelp before furnish gates). Could instead be drift. Either way it's one decision that resolves 24 sites. *(Minor peer-divergence: `buut_cli.sh` resolves `buc_command` via `${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}` instead of `${BURD_BUK_DIR}`.)*

## Confirmed mechanical violations (high confidence)

- **`local -i`** (prohibited — silent int coercion): `rbfcg_GarRest.sh:75`
- **Multi-var `local` on one line** (hidden init failures): `rbfcg_GarRest.sh:74` (6 vars), `rbrn_regime.sh:159`, `buc_command.sh:338`, `bujb_jurisdiction.sh:672`
- **`test … && break/continue/return`** (invert, use `||`): `rbgo_OAuth.sh:215`, `bujp_preflight.sh:73`, `bujb_jurisdiction.sh:1745`, `:1769`
- **`local VAR=$(func)`** (combines decl+capture, swallows exit; also non-`_capture` fn in `$()`): `rbrn_regime.sh:168,169,262`
- **`[[ ]]` for simple/numeric compares** (no pattern → should be `test`): `rbgg_Governor.sh:871,904` · `rbrn_cli.sh:71` · `rbrn_regime.sh:171,270` · `rbq_Qualify.sh:144,165` · `rbz_zipper.sh:276,300` · `rbndb_base.sh:301` · `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:1123` · `bujb_jurisdiction.sh:1010,1689,1756,1761` (~16). *Softer, lower priority:* glob/substring `[[ == *x* ]]` uses (rbgp_Payor, rbgo_OAuth:381,393, rbndb_base:98, rbfd:1114) — legitimate pattern-matching, but BCG prefers `case`.
- **Evicted utilities in modules**: `rbob_bottle.sh:188` (`grep|head|cut` pipe + `|| true`) · `buq_qualify.sh:203` (`awk`). *Borderline:* `rbcc_Constants.sh:174` (`tr` for case-folding — no clean bash 3.2 builtin; may be justified).

## `2>/dev/null` — stderr suppression (BCG: redirect to temp file)

~40 in-scope, **heavily concentrated in `rbfcp_Plumb.sh` (~23)** — a best-effort provenance-display module where nearly every `jq` is `2>/dev/null || true`. Smaller clusters: `rbfcb_BuildHost.sh` (date GNU/BSD fallback ×5), `rbfcg_GarRest` (2), `rbgp_Payor` (2), `rbob_bottle` (2), scattered singletons.
**Not violations (filtered):** `test … 2>/dev/null` for numeric validation is *BCG-blessed* (e.g. `bujb_jurisdiction.sh:758`); terminal probes `stty/tty 2>/dev/null` (`bul_launcher`) and trap `kill 0 2>/dev/null` (`rboo_observe:93`) are Pale/idiomatic.

## Two things worth knowing for the repair pace

1. **The fresh foundry-explosion clusters are the worst offenders.** `local -i`, multi-var `local`, and the `2>/dev/null` swarm all landed in the Jun-4 decomposition (`rbfcg`, `rbfcp`, `rbfcb`). The repair overlaps the foundry-explosion heat's territory.
2. **This was a *greppable-prohibition* sweep — not exhaustive.** It did **not** cover: bare/unbraced `$var` (too noisy to grep cleanly), `A && B || C` pseudo-ternary, kindle-constant-outside-kindle, sentinel-first-line, `if regular_function` (`set -e` suppression), array `set -u` safety, or **decomposition-conformance** of the new modules against the section I just wrote (notably the `k`-form entries `rbldk_/rbfck_/rbflk_`, which are now FM-002 legacy by my own wording). Any of those could be a second sweep.

Want me to go deeper on any band (e.g., run the decomposition-conformance check, or the unbraced-`$var` pass) before you slate — or is this enough to docket?

message.content: Hmm.  You present me with a wall of text when I expected a list of violators.  I do like some of the concerns you raise, but in attempting to overachive, you've upset my process by raising things I didn't ask for.  Lets start with: what is the list of violators?  Then list the category errors you also picked up on, tersely.

content.text:
**Violators** (file — violation · lines)

- `rbfcg_GarRest.sh` — `local -i` (75); multi-var `local` (74); `2>/dev/null` (182,190)
- `rbfcp_Plumb.sh` — `2>/dev/null ||true` ×~23 (70,79,160,180,250,…,622)
- `rbfcb_BuildHost.sh` — `2>/dev/null` ×5 (98,145,146,149,150)
- `rbrn_regime.sh` — `local=$(func)` (168,169,262); multi-var `local` (159); `[[ ]]` numeric (171,270)
- `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` — `&& continue` (1745,1769); `[[ ]]` simple (1010,1689,1756,1761); multi-var `local` (672)
- `rbgg_Governor.sh` — `[[ ]]` simple (871,904); `2>/dev/null` (811)
- `rbq_Qualify.sh` — `[[ ]]` simple (144,165)
- `rbz_zipper.sh` — `[[ ]]` simple (276,300)
- `rbrn_cli.sh` — `[[ ]]` simple (71)
- `rbndb_base.sh` — `[[ ]]` simple (301)
- `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh` — `[[ ]]` simple (1123)
- `rbgo_OAuth.sh` — `&& break` (215); `2>/dev/null` (299)
- `bujp_preflight.sh` — `&& return` (73)
- `buc_command.sh` — multi-var `local` (338)
- `rbob_bottle.sh` — `grep|head|cut`+`||true` (188); `2>/dev/null` (489,534)
- `buq_qualify.sh` — `awk` (203)
- `rbgp_Payor.sh` — `2>/dev/null` (831,1666)
- smaller `2>/dev/null` singletons: `rbflw_Wrest.sh`:64, `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh`:273, `rbge_Rest.sh`:52, `rblm_cli.sh`:303, `bud_dispatch.sh`:233

**Category-level items I also raised** (terse)

- `buym_yelp.sh` at CLI header — ~24 of 33 CLIs; doc-vs-practice, not a per-file bug
- Soft `[[ == *glob* ]]` pattern-matches (rbgp_Payor, rbgo_OAuth:381/393, rbndb_base:98) — tolerated, BCG prefers `case`
- `tr` case-fold `rbcc_Constants.sh:174` — no bash-3.2 builtin; maybe justified
- Out-of-scope (excluded): `rbgj*/` GCB step scripts, `vov_veiled/{ABANDONED,FUTURE}`
- Defensible `2>/dev/null` (not counted): `test … 2>/dev/null` numeric, terminal probes, trap `kill`
- False positives filtered: bare-`>` continuation redirects, `$(( ))` arithmetic, `openssl …-base64`
- Not swept: unbraced `$var`, `A&&B||C`, decomposition-conformance (`k`-form entries), kindle-outside-kindle

lastPrompt: Hmm.  You present me with a wall of text when I expected a list of violators.  I do like some of the concerns you raise, but in attempting to overachive, you've upset my process by raising things I di…

message.content: Hmm.  I expected a list of the specific bash modules that require incorporation of the 0 trick in naming the cli and also the 'main' submodule with the 'kindle' function in it.  Did you identify these?

content.text: Honest answer: no — I noticed `rbldk_/rbfck_/rbflk_` in passing and parked them under "not swept (decomposition-conformance)," but I never did the systematic scan. That's the list you actually wanted, and I buried it. Let me produce it properly now — find every module whose kindle lives in a separate submodule, and check the CLI name and the kindle-file name against the 0-trick.

input.command:
ROOT=/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster
echo "############ Every kindle-bearing file (where each module's kindle lives) ############"
grep -rlE '^z[a-z0-9_]+_kindle\(\)' --include='*.sh' "$ROOT/Tools/rbk" "$ROOT/Tools/buk" \
  | grep -vE 'ABANDONED|/FUTURE/' | sed "s#$ROOT/##" | sort
echo
echo "############ Decomposed modules: prefix groups with a CLI + >=2 body files ############"
for cli in $(find "$ROOT/Tools/rbk" "$ROOT/Tools/buk" -name '*_cli.sh' | grep -vE 'ABANDONED|/FUTURE/'); do
  dir=$(dirname "$cli"); base=$(basename "$cli")
  pfx=${base%_cli.sh}; pfx=${pfx%0}
  files=$(ls "$dir" | grep -E "^${pfx}([0a-z]+)?_.*\.sh$")
  n=$(printf '%s\n' "$files" | grep -c .)
  if [ "$n" -ge 3 ]; then
    printf '=== prefix %-6s (%s files) cli=%s\n' "$pfx" "$n" "$base"
    printf '%s\n' "$files" | sed 's/^/      /'
  fi
done

# jjezs_slate kindle-entry-0trick-renames

## Character
Mechanical rename + source-line fixups per module. Low judgment, except confirming each rbh0 module's gestalt ModuleName (guessed below).

## Goal
Bring all 6 decomposed modules' kindle "main submodule" to the BCG 0-trick entry form `«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh` (the single inclusion-guard + whole kindle). Every CLI already conforms (`«prefix»0_cli.sh`); only the kindle entry file is non-conforming. Authority: BCG "Module Decomposition" + Fading Memory FM-002.

## Renames
Group 1 — k-form (FM-002 direct):
- rbldk_Kindle.sh → rbld0_Lode.sh
- rbfck_Kindle.sh → rbfc0_FoundryCore.sh
- rbflk_Kindle.sh → rbfl0_FoundryLedger.sh

- rbhob_base.sh → rbho0_Onboarding.sh
- rbhpb_base.sh → rbhp0_Payor.sh
- rbhwb_base.sh → rbhw0_Windows.sh

## Per rename
Rename the file, then `grep` the OLD basename across the tree to find every sourcer and update each `source` line. Sourcers cross modules: `rbfck_Kindle.sh` is sourced by `rbldk_Kindle.sh` as well as its own `rbfc0_cli.sh`, so the grep-for-sourcers step is load-bearing, not a single-line edit.

## Cinched
- CLIs already 0-form — touch them only for the source-line edit.
- rbh0 `0` slot already holds content landings (`rbho0_start_here.sh`, `rbhw0_top.sh`); the renamed kindle entry is an ADDITIONAL 0-file, not a replacement for those.
- No behavior change — pure rename + sourcing-path edits.

## Done
All 6 kindle entries are `«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh`; every sourcer updated (verified by grepping the old basenames → zero hits); `rbk-claude-acronyms.md` RBLDK/RBFCK/RBFLK + rbh*b entries updated to the new names; the relevant suite is green (dogfight + lode-lifecycle exercise rbld/rbfc/rbfl; handbook display smoke if one exists for rbh0).
